    • Learning Reflection

Hands-on Exercise 4a

Visualising Distribution

Author

Jedidiah Asaf T

Overview

Overview

This hands-on exercise focuses on visualising distribution using ridgeline plots and raincloud plots.

Setup

Code 
if (!require(pacman)) install.packages("pacman")

pacman::p_load(
  ggdist,
  ggridges,
  ggthemes,
  colorspace,
  tidyverse
)

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

Data Preview

Code 
glimpse(exam)
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…

1. Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot

1.1 Basic Ridgeline Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS
  )
) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = colorspace::lighten("#7097BB", 0.3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

1.2 Ridgeline Plot with Gradient Fill

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = after_stat(x)
  )
) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "English score",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.3 Ridgeline Plot with Tail Probability

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Tail probability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.4 Ridgeline Plot with Quartile Lines

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = factor(after_stat(quantile))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.5 Ridgeline Plot with 95% Probability Range

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = factor(after_stat(quantile))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

2. Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot

2.1 Half-eye Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Half-eye Plot of English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.2 Half-eye Plot with Boxplot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.3 Half-eye Plot with Boxplot and Dot Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth = 0.5,
    dotsize = 2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Raincloud-style Distribution of English Scores",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.4 Final Raincloud Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth = 0.5,
    dotsize = 1.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist() +
  labs(
    title = "Raincloud Plot of English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )
Warning: The provided binwidth will cause dots to overflow the boundaries of the
geometry.
→ Set `binwidth = NA` to automatically determine a binwidth that ensures dots
  fit within the bounds,
→ OR set `overflow = "compress"` to automatically reduce the spacing between
  dots to ensure the dots fit within the bounds,
→ OR set `overflow = "keep"` to allow dots to overflow the bounds of the
  geometry without producing a warning.
ℹ For more information, see the documentation of the `binwidth` and `overflow`
  arguments of `?ggdist::geom_dots()` or the section on constraining dot sizes
  in vignette("dotsinterval") (`vignette(ggdist::dotsinterval)`).

Learning Reflection

In this exercise, I learned that ridgeline plots are useful for comparing score distributions across many groups, while raincloud plots combine density, boxplot, and raw observation views into one chart.

This hands-on exercise focuses on visualising distribution using ridgeline plots and raincloud plots.

Setup

Code 
if (!require(pacman)) install.packages("pacman")

pacman::p_load(
  ggdist,
  ggridges,
  ggthemes,
  colorspace,
  tidyverse
)

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

Data Preview

Code 
glimpse(exam)
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…

1. Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot

1.1 Basic Ridgeline Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS
  )
) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = colorspace::lighten("#7097BB", 0.3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

1.2 Ridgeline Plot with Gradient Fill

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = after_stat(x)
  )
) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "English score",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.3 Ridgeline Plot with Tail Probability

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Tail probability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.4 Ridgeline Plot with Quartile Lines

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = factor(after_stat(quantile))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

1.5 Ridgeline Plot with 95% Probability Range

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = ENGLISH,
    y = CLASS,
    fill = factor(after_stat(quantile))
  )
) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
Picking joint bandwidth of 3.18

2. Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot

2.1 Half-eye Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Half-eye Plot of English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.2 Half-eye Plot with Boxplot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.3 Half-eye Plot with Boxplot and Dot Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth = 0.5,
    dotsize = 2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Raincloud-style Distribution of English Scores",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )

2.4 Final Raincloud Plot

Code 
ggplot(
  exam,
  aes(
    x = RACE,
    y = ENGLISH
  )
) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth = 0.5,
    dotsize = 1.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist() +
  labs(
    title = "Raincloud Plot of English Scores by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "English score"
  )
Warning: The provided binwidth will cause dots to overflow the boundaries of the
geometry.
→ Set `binwidth = NA` to automatically determine a binwidth that ensures dots
  fit within the bounds,
→ OR set `overflow = "compress"` to automatically reduce the spacing between
  dots to ensure the dots fit within the bounds,
→ OR set `overflow = "keep"` to allow dots to overflow the bounds of the
  geometry without producing a warning.
ℹ For more information, see the documentation of the `binwidth` and `overflow`
  arguments of `?ggdist::geom_dots()` or the section on constraining dot sizes
  in vignette("dotsinterval") (`vignette(ggdist::dotsinterval)`).

Learning Reflection

In this exercise, I learned that ridgeline plots are useful for comparing score distributions across many groups, while raincloud plots combine density, boxplot, and raw observation views into one chart.
